The tool query_db was executed successfully. The result is stored under key: var_functions.query_db:6 The result is: [{"rating": "4", "title": "Ha! On me! I thought this was a cookbook!", "text": "Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. Been looking for something like this for a long time.", "text": "Looked online for years for something like this. It's the best I've seen.", "review_time": "2020-08-12 11:06:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_8"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Referance Guide", "text": "Good reference guide for the basics", "review_time": "2014-11-13 18:55:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_178"}]

messages.content:
You are a data analysis agent. Use only the tools listed below to answer the user's query, based on the provided DATABASE DESCRIPTION for logical database names and their types (SQL or MongoDB), and the results of previous tool calls.

TOOLS (system will execute):
- query_db: run a SQL or Mongo query. Returns a list of JSON-serializable records or an error string.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>", "query": "<SQL or Mongo query>"}
- list_db: list tables or collections for a given database.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>"}
- execute_python: run Python code.
  Required args: {"code": "<python_code>"}
- return_answer: finish and return the final answer (plain text).
  Required args: {"answer": "<final plain-text answer>"}

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. After each tool call, its result will be stored in a storage under a key named after the tool call id (you will be told the key name). The next message will include both the result (or a preview if it's large) and the storage key name.
2. Inside execute_python code you may read storage entries using the provided key names, e.g., if the tool call id is 'functions.tool:id', you can access its result via `locals()['var_functions.tool:id']` in your code.
3. You cannot modify or reassign those storage-provided variables; you may read them and create new variables as needed.
4. If a tool result is large, the next message will include a preview (first 10000 characters) and the storage entry will be the .json file path (a string) where the full result is stored. To access the full result, your execute_python code must open and read that .json file.

KEY RULES (must follow exactly):
1. Always use tool calls. Do not output plain text, explanations, or reasoning.
2. Include all required arguments for the tool you call.
3. For query_db, always specify db_name and query. Refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION for db_name and query format.
4. For PostgreSQL, wrap mixed-case or uppercase column names in double quotes.
5. For list_db, refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION to specify db_name.
6. Use execute_python for data processing as needed. 
7. When using execute_python, your code will be quoted by triple double-quotes and passed as a string to `exec(...)` for execution in a Python 3.12 environment with only pandas and pyarrow installed. So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. Do not use non-built-in or non-installed packages.
8. When using execute_python, your code must print the result at the end exactly as shown in the PRINT FORMAT section below. The printed result must be a string that can be successfully parsed by json.loads() without errors.

PRINT FORMAT (must match exactly):
----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----
print("__RESULT__:")
print(your_json_serializable_string_here)
----END PRINT FORMAT----

For simple types (int, float, str, bool, None), you may use json.dumps() to produce a valid JSON string.
For complex or non-JSON-serializable types, you must convert them into JSON-compatible forms before printing.
For lists or dictionaries, you must ensure that all nested elements are also converted into JSON-serializable types.
9. Return the final answer only via a single return_answer tool call. Do not include extra text, explanation, or formatting.

EXAMPLES:
- query_db:
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "SELECT * FROM some_table LIMIT some_limit;"}} # for SQL databases
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "{"collection": "some_collection", "filter": {some_filter}, "projection": {some_projection}, "limit": some_limit}"}} # for MongoDB databases

- list_db:
{"tool": "list_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name"}}

- execute_python:
{"tool": "execute_python", "args": {"code": "import pandas as pd
# rl1 and rl2 are the keys of two JSON-serializable record lists in storage
df1 = pd.DataFrame(rl1)
df2 = pd.DataFrame(rl2)
result = pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id').head(10).to_json(orient='records')
print('__RESULT__:')
print(result)"}}

- return_answer:
{"tool": "return_answer", "args": {"answer": "…final plain-text answer…"}}

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Which English-language books in the 'Literature & Fiction' category have a perfect average rating of 5.0? Return all matching books.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review_database:
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Book title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

2. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review_query.db refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_database.

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. - bookid_1, bookid_2, bookid_3, etc. in books_info
- purchaseid_186, purchaseid_191, purchaseid_190, etc. in review

These don't seem to match at all. Maybe the hint means something else. Let me think...

Actually, maybe I'm misunderstanding the relationship. Let me check if there's any overlap in titles between the two tables, or if I need to extract book_id from purchase_id somehow.

Looking at the review table data, I see it has a "title" field which is the book title from the review. And books_info also has a "title" field. Maybe I should join on title instead?

But that would be problematic because titles might not be exact matches due to different formatting.

Let me re-read the hint: "The fields 'book_id' in books_info and 'purchase_id' in review_query.db refer to the same book entities across different tables."

Actually, I think there might be an issue with the data or the hint. Looking at the sample values:
- book_id: "bookid_1", "bookid_2", "bookid_3"
- purchase_id: "purchaseid_186", "purchaseid_191", "purchaseid_190"


I'll focus on a direct query strategy. Since the IDs don't match, I'll explore alternative approaches to identify potential connections between the tables. The key challenge is finding a reliable method to link books across these two datasets when the primary identifiers don't align.

My next step involves carefully examining the available fields in both tables, looking for common attributes that could serve as linking mechanisms. I'll investigate the "title" field as a potential joining point, while remaining cautious about exact matching issues.

The goal is to construct a query that can effectively cross-reference book information despite the inconsistent ID formats. I'll experiment with different joining techniques to see what insights emerge from connecting these disparate data sources.

messages.content:
You are a data analysis agent. Use only the tools listed below to answer the user's query, based on the provided DATABASE DESCRIPTION for logical database names and their types (SQL or MongoDB), and the results of previous tool calls.

TOOLS (system will execute):
- query_db: run a SQL or Mongo query. Returns a list of JSON-serializable records or an error string.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>", "query": "<SQL or Mongo query>"}
- list_db: list tables or collections for a given database.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>"}
- execute_python: run Python code.
  Required args: {"code": "<python_code>"}
- return_answer: finish and return the final answer (plain text).
  Required args: {"answer": "<final plain-text answer>"}

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. After each tool call, its result will be stored in a storage under a key named after the tool call id (you will be told the key name). The next message will include both the result (or a preview if it's large) and the storage key name.
2. Inside execute_python code you may read storage entries using the provided key names, e.g., if the tool call id is 'functions.tool:id', you can access its result via `locals()['var_functions.tool:id']` in your code.
3. You cannot modify or reassign those storage-provided variables; you may read them and create new variables as needed.
4. If a tool result is large, the next message will include a preview (first 10000 characters) and the storage entry will be the .json file path (a string) where the full result is stored. To access the full result, your execute_python code must open and read that .json file.

KEY RULES (must follow exactly):
1. Always use tool calls. Do not output plain text, explanations, or reasoning.
2. Include all required arguments for the tool you call.
3. For query_db, always specify db_name and query. Refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION for db_name and query format.
4. For PostgreSQL, wrap mixed-case or uppercase column names in double quotes.
5. For list_db, refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION to specify db_name.
6. Use execute_python for data processing as needed. 
7. When using execute_python, your code will be quoted by triple double-quotes and passed as a string to `exec(...)` for execution in a Python 3.12 environment with only pandas and pyarrow installed. So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. Do not use non-built-in or non-installed packages.
8. When using execute_python, your code must print the result at the end exactly as shown in the PRINT FORMAT section below. The printed result must be a string that can be successfully parsed by json.loads() without errors.

PRINT FORMAT (must match exactly):
----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----
print("__RESULT__:")
print(your_json_serializable_string_here)
----END PRINT FORMAT----

For simple types (int, float, str, bool, None), you may use json.dumps() to produce a valid JSON string.
For complex or non-JSON-serializable types, you must convert them into JSON-compatible forms before printing.
For lists or dictionaries, you must ensure that all nested elements are also converted into JSON-serializable types.
9. Return the final answer only via a single return_answer tool call. Do not include extra text, explanation, or formatting.

EXAMPLES:
- query_db:
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "SELECT * FROM some_table LIMIT some_limit;"}} # for SQL databases
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "{"collection": "some_collection", "filter": {some_filter}, "projection": {some_projection}, "limit": some_limit}"}} # for MongoDB databases

- list_db:
{"tool": "list_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name"}}

- execute_python:
{"tool": "execute_python", "args": {"code": "import pandas as pd
# rl1 and rl2 are the keys of two JSON-serializable record lists in storage
df1 = pd.DataFrame(rl1)
df2 = pd.DataFrame(rl2)
result = pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id').head(10).to_json(orient='records')
print('__RESULT__:')
print(result)"}}

- return_answer:
{"tool": "return_answer", "args": {"answer": "…final plain-text answer…"}}

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Which English-language books in the 'Literature & Fiction' category have a perfect average rating of 5.0? Return all matching books.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review_database:
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Book title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

2. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review_query.db refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_database.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book.   Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>"}
- execute_python: run Python code.

- return_answer: finish and return the final answer (plain text).
  Required args: {"answer": "<final plain-text answer>"}

1. After each tool call, its result will be stored in a storage under a key named after the tool call id (you will be told the key name). The next message will include both the result (or a preview if it's large) and the storage key name.
2. Inside execute_python code you may read storage entries using the provided key names, e.g., if the tool call id is 'functions.tool:id', you can access its result via `locals()['var_functions.tool:id']` in your code.
3. You cannot modify or reassign those storage-provided variables; you may read them and create new variables as needed.
4. If a tool result is large, the next message will include a preview (first 10000 characters) and the storage entry will be the .json file path (a string) where the full result is stored. To access the full result, your execute_python code must open and read that .json file.

KEY RULES (must follow exactly):
1. Always use tool calls. Do not output plain text, explanations, or reasoning.
2. Include all required arguments for the tool you call.

5. For list_db, refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION to specify db_name.
6. Use execute_python for data processing as needed.

So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. Do not use non-built-in or non-installed packages.
8. When using execute_python, your code must print the result at the end exactly as shown in the PRINT FORMAT section below. The printed result must be a string that can be successfully parsed by json.loads() without errors.

PRINT FORMAT (must match exactly):
----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----

----END PRINT FORMAT----

For complex or non-JSON-serializable types, you must convert them into JSON-compatible forms before printing.
For lists or dictionaries, you must ensure that all nested elements are also converted into JSON-serializable types.

QUERY:
Which English-language books in the 'Literature & Fiction' category have a perfect average rating of 5.0? Return all matching books.

You are working with two databases to solve this query.

1. review_database

    - review_database:

        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted

2. books_database

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. Been looking for something like this for a long time.", "text": "Looked online for years for something like this. function.arguments: {"db_name": "review_database", "query": "SELECT rating, purchase_id FROM review WHERE purchase_id LIKE '%1' LIMIT 10;"}
function.name: query_db

message.reasoning:
There are 40 books in the Literature & Fiction category. Now I need to find which of these have an average rating of 5.0. Since the rating data is in the review_database and book identification is via purchase_id in review and book_id in books_info, I need to:

1. Get all Literature & Fiction books
2. Query the review database for ratings for each book (using some matching between purchase_id and book_id)
3. Calculate the average rating for each book
4. Filter those with average rating = 5.0

The tricky part is the fuzzy join between purchase_id in review table and book_id in books_info. Looking at samples:
- book_id looks like: "bookid_1", "bookid_9", etc.
- purchase_id looks like: "purchaseid_186", "purchaseid_191", etc.

They seem to follow a pattern where purchase_id = "purchaseid_" + number, and book_id = "bookid_" + number. Let me verify if these numbers match.

Let me first fetch all the review data to see if I can match them. But that might be very large. Instead, let me look at reviews for specific books.

Let me try to query reviews for a specific purchase_id pattern and see if I can match with book_id.

Actually, let me look for reviews where purchase_id contains the same number as book_id. For example, for book_id "bookid_1", I expect purchase_id to be "purchaseid_1".

Let me query reviews to see the purchase_id format:

messages.content:
You are a data analysis agent. Use only the tools listed below to answer the user's query, based on the provided DATABASE DESCRIPTION for logical database names and their types (SQL or MongoDB), and the results of previous tool calls.

TOOLS (system will execute):
- query_db: run a SQL or Mongo query. Returns a list of JSON-serializable records or an error string.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>", "query": "<SQL or Mongo query>"}
- list_db: list tables or collections for a given database.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>"}
- execute_python: run Python code.
  Required args: {"code": "<python_code>"}
- return_answer: finish and return the final answer (plain text).
  Required args: {"answer": "<final plain-text answer>"}

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. After each tool call, its result will be stored in a storage under a key named after the tool call id (you will be told the key name). The next message will include both the result (or a preview if it's large) and the storage key name.
2. Inside execute_python code you may read storage entries using the provided key names, e.g., if the tool call id is 'functions.tool:id', you can access its result via `locals()['var_functions.tool:id']` in your code.
3. You cannot modify or reassign those storage-provided variables; you may read them and create new variables as needed.
4. If a tool result is large, the next message will include a preview (first 10000 characters) and the storage entry will be the .json file path (a string) where the full result is stored. To access the full result, your execute_python code must open and read that .json file.

KEY RULES (must follow exactly):
1. Always use tool calls. Do not output plain text, explanations, or reasoning.
2. Include all required arguments for the tool you call.
3. For query_db, always specify db_name and query. Refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION for db_name and query format.
4. For PostgreSQL, wrap mixed-case or uppercase column names in double quotes.
5. For list_db, refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION to specify db_name.
6. Use execute_python for data processing as needed. 
7. When using execute_python, your code will be quoted by triple double-quotes and passed as a string to `exec(...)` for execution in a Python 3.12 environment with only pandas and pyarrow installed. So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. Do not use non-built-in or non-installed packages.
8. When using execute_python, your code must print the result at the end exactly as shown in the PRINT FORMAT section below. The printed result must be a string that can be successfully parsed by json.loads() without errors.

PRINT FORMAT (must match exactly):
----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----
print("__RESULT__:")
print(your_json_serializable_string_here)
----END PRINT FORMAT----

For simple types (int, float, str, bool, None), you may use json.dumps() to produce a valid JSON string.
For complex or non-JSON-serializable types, you must convert them into JSON-compatible forms before printing.
For lists or dictionaries, you must ensure that all nested elements are also converted into JSON-serializable types.
9. Return the final answer only via a single return_answer tool call. Do not include extra text, explanation, or formatting.

EXAMPLES:
- query_db:
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "SELECT * FROM some_table LIMIT some_limit;"}} # for SQL databases
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "{"collection": "some_collection", "filter": {some_filter}, "projection": {some_projection}, "limit": some_limit}"}} # for MongoDB databases

- list_db:
{"tool": "list_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name"}}

- execute_python:
{"tool": "execute_python", "args": {"code": "import pandas as pd
# rl1 and rl2 are the keys of two JSON-serializable record lists in storage
df1 = pd.DataFrame(rl1)
df2 = pd.DataFrame(rl2)
result = pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id').head(10).to_json(orient='records')
print('__RESULT__:')
print(result)"}}

- return_answer:
{"tool": "return_answer", "args": {"answer": "…final plain-text answer…"}}

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Which English-language books in the 'Literature & Fiction' category have a perfect average rating of 5.0? Return all matching books.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review_database:
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Book title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

2. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review_query.db refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_database.

The tool query_db was executed successfully. The result is stored under key: var_functions.query_db:6 The result is: [{"rating": "4", "title": "Ha! On me! I thought this was a cookbook!", "text": "Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. Been looking for something like this for a long time.", "text": "Looked online for years for something like this. It's the best I've seen.", "review_time": "2020-08-12 11:06:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_8"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Referance Guide", "text": "Good reference guide for the basics", "review_time": "2014-11-13 18:55:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_178"}]

message.reasoning:
The query is asking for English-language books in the 'Literature & Fiction' category that have a perfect average rating of 5.0.

Based on the data I've seen so far:
1. The categories field in books_info is stored as a string that looks like a list, e.g., "["Books", "Literature & Fiction", "History & Criticism"]"
2. I need to match books in the 'Literature & Fiction' category
3. I need to find books that are in English (from the details field)
4. I need to join with the review table to calculate average ratings by purchase_id/book_id
5. The book_id in books_info is like "bookid_1" and purchase_id in review is like "purchaseid_1" - they share the numeric part

Let me now:
1. Get all books in Literature & Fiction category
2. Extract the numeric ID from book_id
3. Get all reviews
4. Join them to calculate average ratings
5. Filter for average rating = 5.0
6. Check if the book is in English

Let me get all the books first, then process them.

- return_answer: finish and return the final answer (plain text).

4. If a tool result is large, the next message will include a preview (first 10000 characters) and the storage entry will be the .json file path (a string) where the full result is stored. To access the full result, your execute_python code must open and read that .json file.

1. Always use tool calls. Do not output plain text, explanations, or reasoning.
2. Include all required arguments for the tool you call.

5. For list_db, refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION to specify db_name.
6. Use execute_python for data processing as needed. 
7. When using execute_python, your code will be quoted by triple double-quotes and passed as a string to `exec(...)` for execution in a Python 3.12 environment with only pandas and pyarrow installed. So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. Do not use non-built-in or non-installed packages.
8. When using execute_python, your code must print the result at the end exactly as shown in the PRINT FORMAT section below. The printed result must be a string that can be successfully parsed by json.loads() without errors.

PRINT FORMAT (must match exactly):
----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----
print("__RESULT__:")
print(your_json_serializable_string_here)
----END PRINT FORMAT----

For simple types (int, float, str, bool, None), you may use json.dumps() to produce a valid JSON string.
For complex or non-JSON-serializable types, you must convert them into JSON-compatible forms before printing.
For lists or dictionaries, you must ensure that all nested elements are also converted into JSON-serializable types.

- query_db:
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "SELECT * FROM some_table LIMIT some_limit;"}} # for SQL databases

# rl1 and rl2 are the keys of two JSON-serializable record lists in storage

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Which English-language books in the 'Literature & Fiction' category have a perfect average rating of 5.0? Return all matching books.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review_database:
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Book title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

2. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review_query.db refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_database.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. Been looking for something like this for a long time.", "text": "Looked online for years for something like this. message.content: Let me systematically explore the data to find English-language Literature & Fiction books with perfect 5.0 average ratings.

function.arguments: {"db_name": "books_database", "query": "SELECT book_id, title, categories, details, rating_number FROM books_info WHERE categories LIKE '%Literature & Fiction%';"}
function.name: query_db

message.reasoning:
The user wants to find English-language books in the 'Literature & Fiction' category with a perfect average rating of 5.0. 

I have:
1. two databases: review_database (SQLite) and books_database (PostgreSQL)
2. review_database has a table "review" with columns: rating, title, text, purchase_id, review_time, helpful_vote, verified_purchase
3. books_database has a table "books_info" with columns: title, subtitle, author, rating_number, features, description, price, store, categories, details, book_id

The hint says:
- description, categories, and features are stored as strings but look like lists/dicts
- book_id in books_info and purchase_id in review_database refer to the same book entities (fuzzy join)
- some queries could use categories or details columns

The challenge is to:
1. Find books with 'Literature & Fiction' in their categories (which is a string representation)
2. Filter to English-language books
3. Determine which have an average rating of 5.0
4. Return all matching books

Since fields are stored as strings but look like lists/dicts, I need to:
- Use string matching to find 'Literature & Fiction' in categories
- Check details for "written in English" or similar language info
- Join review data using book_id and purchase_id to calculate average ratings

Let me start by exploring the data more systematically.

First, let me understand the data structure better:
- In books_info, categories is a string representation like "[\"Books\", \"Literature & Fiction\", \"History & Criticism\"]"
- In books_info, details contains info like "It is written in English"
- In review, purchase_id seems to be like "purchaseid_1" which might correspond to bookid_1

I need to:
1. Get all books in Literature & Fiction category from books_info
2. Filter for English language books
3. Join with review data to calculate average rating
4. Filter for average rating = 5.0

Let me start by getting all books from both tables and then process in Python.

You are a data analysis agent. Use only the tools listed below to answer the user's query, based on the provided DATABASE DESCRIPTION for logical database names and their types (SQL or MongoDB), and the results of previous tool calls.

TOOLS (system will execute):

- execute_python: run Python code.

- return_answer: finish and return the final answer (plain text).

KEY RULES (must follow exactly):
1. Always use tool calls. Do not output plain text, explanations, or reasoning.
2. Include all required arguments for the tool you call.

4. For PostgreSQL, wrap mixed-case or uppercase column names in double quotes.
5. For list_db, refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION to specify db_name.
6. Use execute_python for data processing as needed. 
7. When using execute_python, your code will be quoted by triple double-quotes and passed as a string to `exec(...)` for execution in a Python 3.12 environment with only pandas and pyarrow installed. So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. Do not use non-built-in or non-installed packages.
8. When using execute_python, your code must print the result at the end exactly as shown in the PRINT FORMAT section below. The printed result must be a string that can be successfully parsed by json.loads() without errors.

PRINT FORMAT (must match exactly):
----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----
print("__RESULT__:")
print(your_json_serializable_string_here)
----END PRINT FORMAT----

For simple types (int, float, str, bool, None), you may use json.dumps() to produce a valid JSON string.
For complex or non-JSON-serializable types, you must convert them into JSON-compatible forms before printing.
For lists or dictionaries, you must ensure that all nested elements are also converted into JSON-serializable types.

{"tool": "execute_python", "args": {"code": "import pandas as pd
# rl1 and rl2 are the keys of two JSON-serializable record lists in storage

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Which English-language books in the 'Literature & Fiction' category have a perfect average rating of 5.0? Return all matching books.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review_database:
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Book title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

2. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review_query.db refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_database.

The truth is more twisted than Parker and Knight could have imagined.\"]", "description": "[]", "price": "0.0", "store": "Remington Kane (Author)   Format: Kindle Edition", "categories": "[\"Books\", \"Mystery, Thriller & Suspense\", \"Thrillers & Suspense\"]", "details": "The book was published on May 18, 2014, and is available in English. messages.content:
The tool query_db was executed successfully. The result is stored under key: var_functions.query_db:6 The result is: [{"rating": "4", "title": "Ha! On me! I thought this was a cookbook!", "text": "Lucky for me it's more than a cookbook.

The story of a modern Daniel Boone, growing up in Michigan, one of three sons of a hunter. Beginning at age eight when he shot a squirrel, to age 13 his first deer, then hunting his way through college, a career as a fur trapper, hunting sheep in Alaska, canoeing the Missouri River for deer and ending in Brooklyn age 37. Brooklyn?

I especially liked that I share his disgust at catch & release fishing - if you fish it, you have to eat it - anything else is sadism (my words).

My brother in law is the cook in the family and I wanted to see if this would be a good gift for him and I struck pay dirt following each chapter are \"Tasting Notes\" where the author speaks of cooking wild game.

I also enjoyed it for the history of the land he has hunted as well as the history of the hunt (I too was a big Daniel Boone fan growing up!).

I think more pictures (everything but dead things) would have made this a keeper.", "review_time": "2012-11-24 18:52:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_186"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Four Stars", "text": "Not as developed as Stephanie but I like the characters so far.", "review_time": "2015-12-31 13:35:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_191"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "A wonderful adventure in France", "text": "I loved this book all the way until the end. I have recently discovered that the author is intending to release another book, and from what I understand it will continue where this one left off. I am thankful for this, as the way this book ended was definitely a negative for me. I wanted to know more! What happened to the author, did she learn what she came to learn? Did she get the job she was hoping for? So many questions.

From the beginning of Linda's book, it is easy to love her. She is open, honest and definitely has the type of personality you want your heroine to have, whether the book is fiction or reality. I couldn't help but root for Linda throughout her trials with her host family, even when I thought she acted as a bit of a brat herself. Listening to her internal thoughts about what she went through and her desire to achieve her goals made this book feel close to my heart.

I applaud the adventurous spirit of the author and her decision to keep journal entries from that chapter of her life. What great material to have later to inspire a book! While I enjoyed the entire memoir, my favourite part of this book would have to be the author's descriptions of the many places she visited and the people she met along the way. While I think she could definitely have made a better impression on the family she worked for if she had been honest about her lack of French language skills from the beginning, she is a pioneer. Her drive and desire to learn the language from those in the actual country was inspiring. Not many people would have been gutsy enough to do what Linda did.

I also particularly enjoyed the relationship between Linda and Antoine. The Kind heart of the author was apparent in her actions toward the children, even when she struggled with them.

There is some romance, and I appreciated the way the author handled it. While sex scenes are not necessarily automatically offensive, there is something about memoirs that makes me uncomfortable if they are filled with them. This author manages to hint at her experiences without going into too much detail, leaving the reader to decide exactly what happened. Great writing.

When you step into reading this book, you learn a lot about French customs, French cuisine and wine. You also get the opportunity to brush up on your French skills a bit and get to step outside yourself and live an adventure through the eyes of the author. I read this book in one sitting with very few breaks in between and found myself enthralled in the sights, sounds and atmosphere of this memoir.

In the end, I was disappointed that the book ended. I could have kept reading about Linda's experiences without stopping anytime in the foreseeable future. The end of the book leaves many unanswered questions, that I am assuming the author will be answering in her next book. Were this a standalone, I would be very disappointed with the way the book ended, but with the knowledge that she is writing another book to continue with her story, I can't fault this one. One thing is for sure, if you read this book, you will be anxiously awaiting the next.

I would recommend this to anyone who likes books about travel, memoirs or just a good story that came from the recollections and heart of the author. Read it, you will be glad you did. Overall, this was a VERY enjoyable read that gave me many reasons to wish I could drop everything and head off to France!", "review_time": "2013-05-05 10:47:00", "helpful_vote": "1", "verified_purchase": "0", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_190"}, {"rating": "5", "title": "Best beginner book. Been looking for something like this for a long time.", "text": "Looked online for years for something like this. It's the best I've seen.", "review_time": "2020-08-12 11:06:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_8"}, {"rating": "4", "title": "Referance Guide", "text": "Good reference guide for the basics", "review_time": "2014-11-13 18:55:00", "helpful_vote": "0", "verified_purchase": "1", "purchase_id": "purchaseid_178"}]

function.arguments: {"db_name": "books_database", "query": "SELECT book_id, title, categories, details, rating_number FROM books_info WHERE categories LIKE '%Literature & Fiction%';"}
function.name: query_db

message.reasoning:
The user wants to find English-language books in the "Literature & Fiction" category with a perfect average rating of 5.0.

From the database description:
1. **review_database** (SQLite): Contains individual reviews with ratings (1.0-5.0 scale), linked by purchase_id
2. **books_database** (PostgreSQL): Contains book information including categories and rating_number (total number of ratings), linked by book_id

The hint states that book_id in books_info and purchase_id in review refer to the same book entities, but they're not exact matches - they need a "fuzzy join approach". Looking at the sample data:
- book_id format: "bookid_1", "bookid_2", etc.
- purchase_id format: "purchaseid_186", "purchaseid_191", etc.

It seems the pattern is: bookid_X in books_info corresponds to purchaseid_X in review, where X is the same number.

So I need to:
1. Extract all books in "Literature & Fiction" category from books_info
2. Check if they're English-language (from details field)
3. Join with review table by mapping bookid_X to purchaseid_X
4. Calculate average rating for each book
5. Filter for books with average rating = 5.0

Let me break this down:

**Step 1**: Get all books in Literature & Fiction category from books_database
- Query: SELECT book_id, title, categories, details, rating_number FROM books_info WHERE categories LIKE '%Literature & Fiction%'

**Step 2**: Get all reviews from review_database
- Query: SELECT rating, purchase_id FROM review

**Step 3**: Process in Python to:
- Extract book number from book_id (e.g., "bookid_1" → 1)
- Extract purchase number from purchase_id (e.g., "purchaseid_186" → 186)
- Match them up
- Filter for English-language books (check details field for "English")
- Calculate average rating per book
- Filter for average rating = 5.0

Let me start by getting all the data.

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Which English-language books in the 'Literature & Fiction' category have a perfect average rating of 5.0? Return all matching books.

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. review_database
   - This database is stored in a SQLite database and contains Amazon book review information including ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - review_database:
      - Fields:
        - rating (float): Rating given by reviewer (1.0-5.0 scale)
        - title (str): Book title
        - text (str): Review text content
        - purchase_id (str): Unique identifier linking to book_id in books_database
        - review_time (str): Timestamp when review was posted
        - helpful_vote (int): Number of helpful votes received
        - verified_purchase (bool): Whether purchase was verified

2. books_database
   - This database is stored in a PostgreSQL database and contains Amazon book information including descriptions, price, details, title, etc. up to 2023.
   - This database consists of one table:
    - books_info:
      - Fields:
        - title (str): Book title
        - subtitle (str): Book subtitle
        - author (str): Book author(s)
        - rating_number (int): Total number of ratings received
        - features (str): Book features (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - description (str): Book description (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - price (float): Book price
        - store (str): Store information
        - categories (str): Book categories (stored as string representation of list/dict)
        - details (str): Additional book details
        - book_id (str): Unique book identifier

HINTS: 
- In books_info, the "description", "categories", and "features" content appears to be in list or dictionary format, but they are actually stored as strings in the .sql file.
- The fields "book_id" in books_info and "purchase_id" in review_query.db refer to the same book entities across different tables. While the field names are not exact matches, they can be joined using a fuzzy join approach to solve the query.
- For some queries, you could get needed information from "categories" or "details" columns in books_database.
